commands.note: n=46 readout (~24h after launch): median net +8.03%, mean -6.5%, WR 60.9%. ON-TRACK against target_median +8%. Cohort hit n=46/100 in <24h, well ahead of target_days=10 -- broader filter (sum_abs<60 instead of vol 30-60%) is delivering ~3x the trade rate as predicted. Mean negative due to expected loss tail; cumulative SOL P&L will improve once SL gap calibration catches up. Continue to n=100 for promotion check.

n=46 readout: median +2.15% vs target +11% -- predicted HIGHEST median in cohort, instead it's the LOWEST. Auto-status DEGRADING. Mean -3.54% vs base -6.5% (BE IS rescuing the loss tail by ~3pp), but median dropped from +8.03% to +2.15% (-5.8pp) and WR from 60.9% to 50% (-11pp). Diagnosis: BE@+3% is INSIDE the natural noise band of these tokens. Trades that pump to +3% then naturally pull back through entry now hit the BE-stop instead of riding to TP=10. We're catching noise pullbacks, not catastrophic reversals. Killing this strategy and rolling the BE-recalibration cohort (v17) at +5/+7/+9 to find the inflection point. Operator-killed (kill_criterion median<-3 not formally tripped -- bar was set too lenient given the +11 prediction).

n=46 readout: median +7.31%, mean -6.77%, WR 56.5%. Statistically indistinguishable from v16-calm-base (median +8.03%, mean -6.5%). Runner-catch hypothesis NOT validated -- at trail params (act=+5%, dist=3%) the runners aren't being preserved meaningfully more than TP=10 already captures. Either: (a) runners are too rare in this filter slice, (b) trail activates too early on false starts, or (c) dist=3% is too tight and trails out before runner runs. Continue to n=100 for the proper promotion check, but don't expect divergence from base. If still flat at n=100, runner mechanic needs different params (act=+8%/dist=5% would be the natural follow-up).

commands.hypothesis: Same calm-survivor entry as v16-calm-base, plus a breakeven stop at +3%. Hypothesis: ~25% of trades currently round-trip through the +5-7% zone before failing -- moving the SL to entry once price ticks +3% rescues those near-misses, converting ~10pp of loss tail to flat outcomes. Should produce the highest median of the v16 cohort. Exit-mechanic test: same filter as base, only the BE varies, so the median delta vs base is the BE rescue effect cleanly measured.

v16-calm-be3 failure analysis: BE@+3% sat inside the +/-5% noise band of these tokens, catching natural pullbacks instead of catastrophic reversals. v17 tests three BE triggers above that band. v17-calm-be5 is the 'just outside the noise band' test -- BE fires after price has moved +5%, which is past typical chop. Expect: (a) less BE-stop interference than be3 (median should recover from +2.15% toward base +8%), (b) some loss-tail rescue still preserved (mean better than base -6.5%). If be5 lands at median +6-7% with mean -3% to -5%, the BE concept works at this calibration. Same entry filter as v16-calm-base for direct comparison.

Sweet-spot hypothesis. BE@+7% activates only after the trade has clearly committed direction (well past chop), but still leaves room for the trade to pull back through entry on a true reversal. Predicted best-of-cohort: (a) median ~equivalent to v16-calm-base (+8%) since BE rarely fires inside the win path, (b) mean meaningfully better than base (-3 to -4% vs -6.5%) because BE rescues the worst losers that pump-then-dump. If this hypothesis is correct, v17-calm-be7 is the v16 successor -- same entry, better cost-adjusted return.

Upper-bound control. BE@+9% triggers only one tick before TP=10 fires -- so the BE almost never has time to actually rescue anything. Expect BE to be near-inert: median and mean both ~equivalent to v16-calm-base. This variant exists to confirm the BE effect is a function of trigger level, not a bug or unrelated factor. If be9 differs meaningfully from base, something else is going on. If be9 ~= base and be7 > base on mean, the BE recalibration thesis is confirmed.
